comment.body: I’ve only followed your Substack since probably the beginning of June, didn’t know these chats were available until this week. First just want to give you some well deserved recognition of what you’ve accomplished studying the entirety of the markets… it is hands down one of the most impressive analysis any scholar has achieved at their study… but you make me laugh in a good way about how you fully grasp the factor that nothing in the markets can be considered with a 100 percent certainty you still dedicate you’re time, energy, and skills to its research.. I guess it goes hand and hand with the old saying “in science nothing can be proven certain, only disproven”… I respect your level of knowledge and madness.. it has given myself a new perspective on how I try to understand the whys and the hows of what I’m researching. Thank you.

user.name: Alma

comment.body:
we recognized that it seems like it reverts to a mean by time to time. whats the idea behind mean reversion? past returns will be rebalanced in the future, and the momentum reverts to its long term average.
okay... but how does it differ from like oscillation for example?
comment.raw_body:
we recognized that it seems like it reverts to a mean by time to time. whats the idea behind mean reversion? past returns will be rebalanced in the future, and the momentum reverts to its long term average.
okay... but how does it differ from like oscillation for example?

user.name: Alma
user.bio: Privat investor & speculator | Mathematics | Self-made quant | Market researcher | Options and Futures trader

comment.body: well, mean reversion is a "rubber band" thing, where we assume that the force of it is proportional to the amount of deviation from the mean, leading to an exponential decay of deviations rather than sustained cycles (like in oscillation)
comment.raw_body: well, mean reversion is a "rubber band" thing, where we assume that the force of it is proportional to the amount of deviation from the mean, leading to an exponential decay of deviations rather than sustained cycles (like in oscillation)

user.name: Alma
user.bio: Privat investor & speculator | Mathematics | Self-made quant | Market researcher | Options and Futures trader

comment.body: however this force can be dispersed over time
comment.raw_body: however this force can be dispersed over time

user.name: Alma
user.bio: Privat investor & speculator | Mathematics | Self-made quant | Market researcher | Options and Futures trader

comment.body:
for continuous time, we can model it via OU process
where we take the rate of the mean reversion, the long term mean, volatility, and the wiener process. it is a nontrivial stationary gauss markov process, having an autovariance that decays exponentially.
comment.raw_body:
for continuous time, we can model it via OU process
where we take the rate of the mean reversion, the long term mean, volatility, and the wiener process. it is a nontrivial stationary gauss markov process, having an autovariance that decays exponentially.

comment.body: traditional models like garch, heston, assume that vol moves smoothly from one point to the next, however in fact vol jump up and down.
comment.raw_body: traditional models like garch, heston, assume that vol moves smoothly from one point to the next, however in fact vol jump up and down.

user.name: Alma
user.bio: Privat investor & speculator | Mathematics | Self-made quant | Market researcher | Options and Futures trader

comment.body: the second problem is that, the classical models assume a longer-term memory, meaning: as the vol spikes, it decays smoothly and slowly... however, in fact its memory is very short, and barely reacts to it (later it does some rebalancing but thats different)...
comment.raw_body: the second problem is that, the classical models assume a longer-term memory, meaning: as the vol spikes, it decays smoothly and slowly... however, in fact its memory is very short, and barely reacts to it (later it does some rebalancing but thats different)...

then based on that, using the distribution, i project potential volatility values for myself, check the hsitroical skewness and kurtosis, its changes, project the next potential values, and so i can assign probabilities to the projected volatility levels.
